chunk_summary: This checklist provides a practical approach for assessing micronutrient deficiencies in children presenting with growth faltering, recurrent infections, or chronic conditions, using risk factors and physical signs before ordering diagnostic tests.
learning_objectives: Identify risk factors and clinical signs for common micronutrient deficiencies in pediatric patients.
Understand the importance of targeted screening for anemia, calcium, vitamin A, iodine, and zinc deficiencies.
Learn to correlate dietary habits and medical history with physical examination findings for micronutrient risk assessment.
key_facts: Anemia risk factors include high cow’s milk intake, prematurity, pica, and worm exposure; signs include pallor, tachycardia, and poor growth.
Bone and calcium deficiency risk factors include low sun exposure, anticonvulsant use, and malabsorption; signs include craniotabes, rachitic rosary, and tetany.
Vitamin A deficiency risk factors include poor dietary diversity and chronic diarrhea; signs include conjunctival xerosis, Bitot spots, and night blindness.
Iodine deficiency risk factors include non-iodized salt use and family history of goiter; signs include dry skin, bradycardia, and goiter.
Zinc deficiency signs include periorificial dermatitis, alopecia, and growth faltering, often linked to high-phytate diets or persistent diarrhea.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Anemia risk screen: high cow’s milk intake, poor complementary feeding, prematurity/LBW, pica, worm exposure, heavy menstrual bleeding, chronic infection/inflammation, family history of hemoglobinopathy. (Source: IAP standard treatment guidelines: Iron deficiency anemia)
